aliases: Beef bowl with cheese and egg

english: Beef bowl with cheese and egg

food_subgroup: Rice bowl set meal with beef, cheese, egg, and soup

ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a general beef rice bowl concept (FOODON_00002809); no specific gyudon-with-cheese entry exists. Closest parent match used.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A0CKW covers beef and rice dishes broadly; no specific gyudon or cheese-topped gyudon code exists in the public FoodEx2 catalogue. Closest parent match used.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT food concept for gyudon or cheese gyudon identified in the public SNOMED CT browser.

serving.description: 1 combo set (gyudon bowl + miso soup or drink)
serving.natural_language_portion: One standard serving is approximately 550 g, comprising a gyudon rice bowl with melted cheese topping (approximately 450 g) and a bowl of miso soup (approximately 100 g).

nutrient_source.title: Gyudon (beef rice bowl) — USDA FoodData Central composite estimate, supplemented with USDA SR Legacy entries for cheddar cheese and half-boiled egg
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service — FoodData Central
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No single USDA or HPB entry exists for cheese gyudon combo. Values are estimated by combining USDA FoodData Central data for gyudon-style beef rice bowl (NDB reference for beef and rice mixed dish), cheddar cheese (USDA SR Legacy #01009), and hard-boiled egg (USDA SR Legacy #01129), weighted to approximate a standard Sukiya-style cheese gyudon serving. Miso soup contribution is included at a diluted weight. Per-100g values represent the blended composite. Confidence is medium due to estimation.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — white rice and mixed beef-rice dishes
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC. Diabetes Care 2008
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for cheese gyudon. GI of 65 is estimated based on the dominant carbohydrate source being white steamed rice (GI ~64–72 per University of Sydney GI Database) and the moderating effect of protein from beef and cheese and fat from cheese, which lower the effective GI slightly below plain white rice. Analogous estimate anchored to white rice GI 65 (medium). Glycemic load calculated as (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (65 × 101.75) / 100 ≈ 66.2, rounded to 66.5 for the 550 g serving containing approximately 101.75 g available carbohydrate.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content from dashi-based sauce, miso soup, and processed cheese
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit or avoid this dish due to its estimated sodium content of approximately 310 mg per 100 g (approximately 1,705 mg per 550 g serving), which represents a substantial portion of the recommended daily sodium limit of 2,000 mg; requesting reduced-sauce and omitting miso soup can lower sodium intake.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High glycemic load from white rice as the dominant carbohydrate source
type2_diabetes.guidance: Individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should be cautious with this dish because the large white rice base produces a high glycemic load (estimated GL ≈ 66 per serving), which can cause significant postprandial blood glucose elevation; portion reduction, substitution with lower-GI rice, or pairing with additional non-starchy vegetables is advisable.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat from melted cheese and beef, combined with moderate cholesterol from egg
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should consume this dish in moderation because the combination of melted cheese, beef, and half-boiled egg contributes approximately 3.1 g saturated fat and 62 mg cholesterol per 100 g, and the high sodium content further elevates cardiovascular risk; limiting portion size and frequency of consumption is recommended.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Melted Cheese Gyudon Combo is a Japanese beef rice bowl dish (gyudon) topped with both shredded and melted cheese, served as a combo set with a half-boiled egg and either miso soup or a drink. Gyudon is a classic Japanese fast-food dish consisting of thinly sliced beef and onions simmered in a sweet-savoury dashi-based sauce (typically containing soy sauce, mirin, and sake) served over a bowl of steamed white rice. In this cheese variant, popularised by Japanese fast-food chains such as Sukiya, a generous layer of shredded cheddar-style cheese is placed over the beef and then melted, adding a creamy, rich, and slightly tangy flavour profile that contrasts with the umami-forward beef topping. The half-boiled egg adds additional protein and a soft, runny yolk that blends into the dish. The miso soup accompaniment provides a warm, savoury complement. Nutritionally, this is a calorie-dense, high-carbohydrate meal dominated by white rice, with meaningful contributions of protein from beef, cheese, and egg, and significant saturated fat and sodium from the cheese and sauce. The dish carries a medium glycemic index (estimated GI ~65) but a high glycemic load per serving due to the large rice portion, making it a concern for individuals managing blood glucose. Sodium content is elevated due to the dashi sauce, cheese, and miso soup, raising concern for those with hypertension or cardiovascular disease. This dish is not suitable for vegetarians, vegans, or those with dairy, egg, wheat, soy, or gluten allergies.
common_user_queries: How many calories in melted cheese gyudon?
What is the sodium content of cheese gyudon?
Is Sukiya cheese gyudon high in fat?
What allergens are in cheese gyudon?
What is the GI of gyudon?

confidence_reason: No exact entry for cheese gyudon combo exists in HPB Singapore Foodo, USDA FoodData Central, or any other approved primary source. Nutrient values are estimated by compositing USDA SR Legacy data for beef rice bowl, cheddar cheese, and half-boiled egg weighted to a standard serving. GI is an analogous estimate anchored to white rice GI from the University of Sydney GI Database. Medium confidence reflects the estimation methodology and absence of a direct source match.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are composite estimates derived from USDA FoodData Central and USDA SR Legacy entries for beef rice bowl, cheddar cheese (SR Legacy #01009), and hard-boiled egg (SR Legacy #01129); no single approved-source entry for cheese gyudon exists. 2. Serving size of 550 g is an estimate based on typical Sukiya gyudon regular-size bowl (~350 g) plus cheese topping (~50 g) plus miso soup (~100 g) plus egg (~50 g). 3. Sodium estimate of 310 mg/100 g reflects the combined contribution of dashi-soy sauce, processed cheese, and miso soup; actual restaurant sodium may be higher. 4. GI of 65 is an analogous estimate based on white rice as the dominant carbohydrate source per University of Sydney GI Database; no direct GI measurement for cheese gyudon is available in approved sources. 5. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, potassium_mg, calcium_mg, iron_mg, cholesterol_mg, and all micronutrients are estimated from USDA SR Legacy component data and are approximate. 6. The food_group in the input JSON was listed as 'Dairy and eggs'; this has been corrected to 'Mixed' as the dish is a composite rice bowl with beef, cheese, egg, and soup. 7. Halal status is unconfirmed; Sukiya Singapore has not been verified as halal-certified at time of processing. 8. FoodOn URI FOODON_00002809 and FoodEx2 code A0CKW are closest_parent matches; no exact ontology code for cheese gyudon exists in the public catalogues reviewed.
